concept.title: Docker for DITA-OT: Local Builds and Reproducible Toolchains
concept.shortdesc: Using the official DITA-OT Docker image for consistent, reproducible builds that eliminate environment differences across developer machines and CI pipelines.

section.title: Why use Docker for DITA-OT builds?
section.p: The official DITA-OT Docker image ensures every build runs in the same environment, regardless of the host machine. This eliminates "it works on my machine" problems and simplifies CI/CD integration.

ul.li: No local Java required: The container includes the correct JDK version
Consistent across machines: Same build environment for every developer
Version pinning: Lock your builds to a specific DITA-OT version
Easy CI/CD: Drop into any container-based pipeline

section.title: How do I get started with Docker builds?
section.p: Run a build by mounting your source directory:

section.title: How do I integrate Docker builds into CI/CD?
section.p: Add the Docker build to your GitHub Actions or Jenkins pipeline. The container handles all dependencies automatically — no Java, no plugin installation, no environment setup required on the build agent.
section.codeblock: # GitHub Actions example - name: Build DITA docs run: | docker run -v ${{ github.workspace }}/docs:/src \ ghcr.io/dita-ot/dita-ot:4.2 \ -i /src/training.ditamap \ -f html5 \ -o /src/output

section.title: What are common issues and tips?

ul.li: Permission errors: Ensure the mounted directory is readable and writable by the container user. On Linux, use --user $(id -u):$(id -g) to match file ownership.
Plugin installation: The base image includes only the default plugins. To add custom plugins, extend the image with a Dockerfile that runs dita install at build time.
Pin versions: Always use a specific tag (e.g., 4.2) rather than latest to ensure reproducible builds.
Build performance: Mount only the source directory, not the entire workspace. Use .dockerignore if building a custom image to reduce context size.
